title: Question 2
stem: A 27 year old man presented with a diffuse maculopapular rash, including on his palms and soles and a 4 cm white-gray raised perianal lesion. A biopsy was done of the perianal lesions and the histology is depicted in the H&E image. Which further testing should be ordered on the biopsy to best help determine the diagnosis?
teaching_point: . T. pallidum IHC. The H&E image demonstrates epithelial hyperplasia with a dense subepithelial plasmacytic infiltrate indicative of condyloma lata. T. pallidum IHC is the most sensitive and specific method to highlight the spirochetes within the tissue. while the Warthin-Starry silver stain may also be used to highlight T. pallidum, it is not as sensitive or specific as IHC for T. pallidum. an AFB stain should be done when chronic granulomatous inflammation is seen to rule out anal tuberculosis. p63 IHC is positive in condyloma accuminata, which will demonstrate koilocytes and should not have any plasmacytic inflammation. IgG4 related disease presents with masses and does not present with a rash. The plasma cells in condyloma lata should also be negative for IgG4 IHC. Anus & perianal area - Syphilis

title: Question 3
stem: What histologic feature separates primary syphilis (chancre) from secondary syphilis (condyloma lata)?
teaching_point: . Epithelial hyperplasia is seen with secondary syphilis lesions (condyloma lata) but not with primary syphilis lesions (chancre), in which ulceration is more common. granulomas are not specific for any stage of syphilis nor specific for syphilis. coagulative necrosis is seen in tertiary syphilis lesions (gummas) but not in chancres or condyloma lata. obliterative endarteritis can be seen in both primary and secondary syphilis. Anus & perianal area - Syphilis

title: Question 6
stem: A 66 year old White woman presents with anal pain and is found to have a pigmented anal mass. The mass is biopsied and the histology is depicted in the H&E image shown above. Which immunohistochemical stain would best support the correct diagnosis?
teaching_point: HMB45. The H&E image demonstrates anal melanoma. HMB45 is a melanocytic marker that would confirm the diagnosis of melanoma, along with the malignant features seen on H&E. CD45 is a lymphocytic marker. CD45 should be ordered to rule out lymphoma but a negative result would not confirm the diagnosis in this case. melanoma is typically pancytokeratin negative. This would be a good confirmatory stain but would not be diagnostic of melanoma by itself. CDX2 is a common marker for colonic adenocarcinomas. CDX2 should be used to rule out a primary rectal adenocarcinoma that involves the anus but will be negative for any anal malignancies, including melanoma. Anal melanoma

title: Question 7
stem: Which of the following characteristics is similar for both cutaneous and anal melanoma?
teaching_point: Staining / IHC. Cutaneous and anal melanoma will stain with similar melanocytic markers (SOX10, S100, HMB45, MelanA, etc.). anal melanoma has a worse prognosis when compared to cutaneous melanoma, especially because patients with anal melanoma often have advanced disease on presentation due to the vague clinical symptoms and difficulty in diagnosis. anal melanoma typically presents with vague symptoms and not the typical ABCDE (asymmetry, border, color, diameter, evolving) criteria for cutaneous melanoma. anal melanoma does not have a standardized pathologic staging system. Anal melanomas can be staged similarly to cutaneous melanoma but this is not universal. Anal melanoma

title: Question 13
stem: A 54 year old woman presented with rectal fullness and was found to have a cystic tumor in the retrorectal space on pelvic magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). The lesion was surgically resected and is shown above. Which of the following, if found on histologic examination, would be most suggestive of a tailgut cyst?
teaching_point: Fibroconnective tissue cyst wall containing disorganized smooth muscle bundles is characteristic of tailgut cysts. an associated well developed smooth muscle layer that is muscularis propria-like and often with nerve plexi is seen in rectal duplication cysts. a cyst lining comprised of attenuated arachnoid cells is featured in anterior sacral meningoceles. keratinizing stratified squamous epithelial lining with associated skin adnexal structures is seen in dermoid cysts. physaliphorous cells showing immunoreactivity for brachyury is found in cystic sacrococcygeal chordomas. Tailgut cyst

title: Question 14
stem: A 59 year old woman presented with constipation and was found to have a cystic tumor in the presacral space. The lesion was surgically resected and submitted for histopathological evaluation. A diagnosis of tailgut cyst was rendered. Which of the following is true regarding the diagnosis?
teaching_point: The prognosis of tailgut cysts depends upon the presence or absence of malignant transformation. It is reported that up to ~25% of tailgut cysts show malignant transformation, most commonly as adenocarcinoma or well differentiated neuroendocrine tumors, although squamous cell carcinoma, adenosquamous carcinoma, transitional cell carcinoma and sarcoma have also been reported. fine needle biopsy is not recommended prior to surgical excision due to risk of infection or seeding of tumor cells. squamous cell carcinoma arising in association with tailgut cyst is extremely rare and limited to case reports. tailgut cysts are typically multilocular (~80%). tailgut cysts show disorganized / irregular smooth muscle bundles without nerve plexi within the cyst wall, as opposed to rectal duplication cysts, which show a well developed smooth muscle layer (muscularis propria-like) with nerve plexi. Tailgut cyst

teaching_point: . Anal Paget disease is generally indolent but may recur. Fewer than half of anal Paget disease cases are associated with invasive malignancy. It does not itself metastasize but it does not spontaneously regress either. Anus & perianal area - Paget disease

teaching_point: HPV negative, p53 aberrant staining. Patients with this subtype have the worst prognosis. HPV positive cancers with p53 wild type staining confer the best prognosis and the other choices have an intermediate prognosis. Squamous cell carcinoma

title: Question 26
stem: What is the preferred treatment for an inflammatory cloacogenic polyp?
teaching_point: . Endoscopic / surgical resection is the preferred treatment for an inflammatory cloacogenic polyp. Routine endoscopic surveillance is necessary after resection because of the risk of recurrence but it is not the treatment modality. Inflammatory cloacogenic polyp

title: Question 27
stem: A 56 year old woman came to the clinic complaining of a lump protruding from her anal opening. It was initially reducible but it is now irreducible. There is associated pain and itching. She also noticed bright red blood on her stool when she defecates. There is an associated history of chronic constipation. Examination of the perianal area revealed skin tags and a tender perianal mass covered with mucosa. Inspection of the anal mucosa showed no fissure. Histologic examination of the perianal mass revealed the image above. What is the most likely diagnosis?
teaching_point: Fourth degree internal hemorrhoid. The image shows thin walled dilated blood vessels with overlying glandular mucosa. external hemorrhoids are covered with squamous mucosa. third degree hemorrhoids are reducible with manual replacement. proctitis does not protrude from the anal opening. Hemorrhoids

title: Question 33
stem: Which of the following features best distinguishes Buschke-Löwenstein tumor from verrucous squamous carcinoma?
teaching_point: . HPV ISH positivity. Buschke-Löwenstein tumor is an HPV related neoplasm whereas verrucous carcinoma is not, so HPV ISH staining (positive) would be expected with Buschke-Löwenstein tumor but not with verrucous carcinoma. Buschke-Löwenstein tumor is most often due to a low risk HPV type, such as HPV 6 or 11. Invasive or displacing type growth can be seen in either lesion. Buschke-Löwenstein tumor

title: Question 34
stem: Lymphadenopathy seen in conjunction with a bulky anovaginal tumor characterized by parakeratosis, superficial koilocytosis, HPV ISH positivity and minimal squamous atypia as seen in this image is most likely due to
teaching_point: . Reactive lymphadenopathy related to tumor associated infection. Buschke-Löwenstein tumor is frequently associated with fistulae and perirectal or peritumoral abscess formation, which can lead to regional lymphadenopathy. Metastasis is very uncommon. While immunocompromise may predispose to development of Buschke-Löwenstein tumor, the cause is infrequently due to a lymphoproliferative disorder that would enlarge the lymph nodes. Netherton syndrome is not primarily associated with adenopathy. Buschke-Löwenstein tumor

title: Question 35
stem: Which of the following immunohistochemical markers are commonly positive in apocrine carcinoma?
teaching_point: GCDFP-15, androgen receptor, CK7. Apocrine carcinoma typically expresses GCDFP-15, AR and CK7. these markers are associated with gastrointestinal adenocarcinomas, not apocrine carcinomas. CDX2 and CK20 are specific to intestinal differentiation, which is not a feature of apocrine tumors. while CK7 is positive in apocrine carcinoma, CK19 is not specific to apocrine differentiation and CDX2 indicates intestinal origin. while GCDFP-15 is typically positive in apocrine carcinoma, ER and PR would likely be negative, differentiating apocrine carcinomas from other hormone sensitive carcinomas, such as breast cancer. Apocrine carcinoma

title: Question 36
stem: What is a distinguishing histologic feature of apocrine carcinoma (shown above)?
teaching_point: Large pleomorphic cells with eosinophilic granular cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli. Apocrine carcinoma is characterized histologically by large pleomorphic cells with eosinophilic granular cytoplasm and prominent nucleoli, which are hallmark features of apocrine differentiation. discohesive large cells with prominent nucleoli, brown pigments and necrosis are more suggestive of melanoma rather than apocrine carcinoma. signet ring cells with mucin production are a defining feature of signet ring cell carcinoma, typically of colorectal or gastric origin, and are not associated with apocrine carcinoma. small blue cells with hyperchromatic nuclei are characteristic of basal cell carcinoma or small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma. These cells are markedly different in size and morphology from the large pleomorphic apocrine cells seen in apocrine carcinoma. Apocrine carcinoma

title: Question 37
stem: A 55 year old woman presents with a painless 2 cm perianal mass, which is excised. It is positive for S100 and CD68. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
teaching_point: Granular cell tumor. The histologic appearance and immunohistochemical profile are consistent with granular cell tumor. Answers A, B and D are incorrect because the other lesions are negative for S100 and CD68. Alveolar soft part sarcoma and squamous cell carcinoma would show more cytologic atypia and gastrointestinal stromal tumor would likely be spindled. Granular cell tumor
